title: Physics: Quantum, Nuclear & Relativity
subtitle: The very small, the very fast & the structure of spacetime
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark paper, experiment or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags replication status, theoretical revisions and known controversies. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 confirmed to high precision (multiple sigma) · 🟡 nuanced, reduced effect or ongoing debate · 🔴 failed or severely contested. Map pins mark the institution where the work was carried out.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: The frameworks that organise quantum mechanics, nuclear structure, and the geometry of spacetime

entries.label: Special Relativity
entries.hint: Physics is the same in all inertial frames and the speed of light is constant in vacuo — from these two postulates Einstein derived time dilation, length contraction, the relativity of simultaneity, and the equivalence of mass and energy. The theory demolished the concept of absolute time and the luminiferous aether in a single stroke.
entries.note: Einstein, A. (1905). 'Zur Elektrodynamik bewegter Körper.' Annalen der Physik, 17, 891–921. Confirmed by thousands of experiments including relativistic mass measurements in particle accelerators, muon lifetime extension at cosmic-ray energies, and GPS clock corrections. The Michelson-Morley experiment (1887) removed the empirical support for absolute space; Special Relativity gave the theoretical resolution.

entries.label: General Relativity
entries.loc: Prussian Academy of Sciences, Berlin, Germany
entries.hint: Gravity is not a force but the curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy, described by the Einstein field equations. General Relativity predicted gravitational lensing, black holes, gravitational waves, and the expanding universe — all confirmed a century after publication.
entries.note: Einstein, A. (1915). 'Die Feldgleichungen der Gravitation.' Sitzungsberichte der Preußischen Akademie der Wissenschaften, 844–847. Confirmed by Eddington's eclipse expedition (1919), Pound-Rebka gravitational redshift (1959), Hulse-Taylor binary pulsar orbital decay (Nobel 1993), and LIGO gravitational waves (2015, Nobel 2017). The cosmological constant, which Einstein introduced to allow a static universe and later called his 'greatest blunder', now models dark energy.

entries.label: Matrix Mechanics
entries.hint: Heisenberg's first complete formulation of quantum mechanics replaced classical trajectories with arrays of numbers — matrices — representing the probabilities of transitions between energy states. It was the first internally consistent quantum theory, though its physical meaning was deeply opaque even to its creator.
entries.note: Heisenberg, W. (1925). 'Über quantentheoretische Umdeutung kinematischer und mechanischer Beziehungen.' Zeitschrift für Physik, 33, 879–893. Formalised as matrix mechanics by Born, M. & Jordan, P. (1925). Zeitschrift für Physik, 34, 858–888. Shown to be mathematically equivalent to Schrödinger's wave mechanics by Schrödinger (1926) Annalen der Physik, 79, 734 and by Dirac (1930) who developed the unifying transformation theory.

entries.label: Wave Mechanics / Schrödinger Equation
entries.hint: The Schrödinger equation describes how a quantum system's wavefunction evolves in time — the square of the wavefunction at each point gives the probability of finding the particle there. It correctly predicts atomic energy levels, molecular structure, and quantum tunnelling, making it the workhorse of quantum chemistry and condensed matter physics.
entries.note: Schrödinger, E. (1926). 'Quantisierung als Eigenwertproblem.' Annalen der Physik, 79, 361–376 (Part I of four). Proved equivalent to matrix mechanics in Part IV (1926). The time-dependent form iℏ ∂ψ/∂t = Ĥψ is universal; the time-independent form Ĥψ = Eψ generates the atomic orbital solutions. Born's probability interpretation (1926) gave the wavefunction its physical meaning and earned him the 1954 Nobel Prize.

entries.label: Copenhagen Interpretation
entries.hint: The dominant interpretation of quantum mechanics: wavefunctions represent probability amplitudes, measurement causes the wavefunction to collapse to a definite outcome, and it is meaningless to ask what properties a system has before measurement. Championed by Bohr and Heisenberg, it was resisted by Einstein, Schrödinger and de Broglie throughout their careers.
entries.note: The interpretation was never written as a single paper; it crystallised at the 1927 Solvay conference. Bohr, N. (1928). 'The Quantum Postulate and the Recent Development of Atomic Theory.' Nature, 121, 580–590. Challenged empirically by Bell's theorem (1964) which ruled out local hidden variables. De Broglie-Bohm pilot wave theory (Bohm 1952, Physical Review, 85, 166) provides a working deterministic alternative at the cost of explicit nonlocality. Copenhagen remains the dominant working interpretation among physicists.

entries.label: Quantum Electrodynamics (QED)
entries.hint: The quantum field theory of the electromagnetic interaction — Feynman, Schwinger, and Tomonaga independently renormalised the infinities that had plagued quantum field theory, producing a framework whose prediction of the electron's anomalous magnetic moment agrees with experiment to 12 decimal places, the most precisely tested prediction in science.
entries.note: Feynman, R. P. (1949). 'Space-Time Approach to Quantum Electrodynamics.' Physical Review, 76, 769–789. Schwinger, J. (1948). Physical Review, 73, 416. Tomonaga, S. (1946). Progress of Theoretical Physics, 1, 27. Dyson, F. J. (1949) Physical Review, 75, 486 proved the three formulations equivalent. Nobel Prize 1965 (Feynman, Schwinger, Tomonaga). The electron magnetic moment: g/2 = 1.00115965218073 (theory) vs 1.00115965218059 ± 0.00000000000013 (experiment).

entries.label: Nuclear Shell Model
entries.hint: Developed independently by Maria Goeppert Mayer at the University of Chicago and J. Hans D. Jensen at Heidelberg, the nuclear shell model proposes that protons and neutrons fill discrete energy shells inside the nucleus, analogous to electron shells in atoms — magic numbers (2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126) mark nuclei with filled shells that are exceptionally stable. Goeppert Mayer won the 1963 Nobel Prize for this work, making her the second woman to win the Physics Nobel after Marie Curie.
entries.note: Goeppert Mayer, M. (1949). 'On Closed Shells in Nuclei.' Physical Review, 75, 1969–1970. Jensen, J. H. D., Suess, H. E. & Haxel, O. (1949) independently arrived at the same model. The critical insight was spin-orbit coupling, which splits the energy levels and produces the observed magic numbers. Nobel Prize in Physics 1963 (Goeppert Mayer and Jensen — Goeppert Mayer was the second woman to win the Physics Nobel, after Marie Curie).

entries.label: The Standard Model
entries.loc: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, Massachusetts
entries.hint: The Standard Model unifies the electromagnetic and weak forces into a single electroweak theory, and incorporates the strong force (QCD) within the same gauge framework (SU(3) × SU(2) × U(1)) — it does not unify the strong force with the electroweak sector (that is the goal of Grand Unified Theories). It describes quarks, leptons and the force-carrying bosons and accounts for virtually every particle physics result obtained at accelerators, from W and Z boson masses to the Higgs field.
entries.note: Weinberg, S. (1967). 'A Model of Leptons.' Physical Review Letters, 19, 1264–1266 (electroweak unification). Glashow, S. L. (1961) provided the gauge structure; Salam, A. (1968) independently constructed the same model. QCD completed by Fritzsch, Gell-Mann & Leutwyler (1973) Physics Letters B, 47, 365. Nobel Prizes: Glashow, Salam, Weinberg 1979; Gross, Politzer, Wilczek 2004. Higgs boson discovered at CERN in 2012 (ATLAS & CMS Collaborations, Science, 338, 1569).

entries.label: Big Bang Cosmology
entries.hint: The universe began in an extremely hot, dense state and has expanded and cooled since — this model predicts the cosmic microwave background (the afterglow of the hot early universe), the abundance ratios of hydrogen and helium, and the large-scale structure of the cosmos, all subsequently confirmed.
entries.note: Alpher, R. A., Bethe, H. & Gamow, G. (1948). 'The Origin of the Chemical Elements.' Physical Review, 73, 803–804. Alpher & Herman (1948) predicted the CMB at ~5 K. CMB discovered: Penzias, A. A. & Wilson, R. W. (1965) Astrophysical Journal, 142, 419 (Nobel 1978). CMB mapped: COBE (Nobel 2006), WMAP, Planck satellite. Inflation (Guth 1981, Physical Review D) extended the model to explain CMB uniformity and flatness.

entries.label: Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD)
entries.hint: The gauge theory of the strong force: quarks carry colour charge (red, green, blue) and interact by exchanging gluons. Asymptotic freedom — quarks behave like free particles at high energies but are permanently confined at low energies — is the key feature; it explains why isolated quarks are never observed and why protons and neutrons are stable.
entries.note: Gross, D. J. & Wilczek, F. (1973). 'Ultraviolet Behavior of Non-Abelian Gauge Theories.' Physical Review Letters, 30, 1343–1346. Politzer, H. D. (1973) independently derived asymptotic freedom. Nobel Prize in Physics 2004. Confinement — the impossibility of isolating a single quark — is mathematically unproven (it is one of the seven Clay Millennium Prize problems) but experimentally incontrovertible: no isolated colour charge has ever been detected.

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: The defining experiments — and exactly what they found

entries.label: Michelson-Morley Experiment
entries.loc: Case School of Applied Science, Cleveland, Ohio
entries.hint: Using a split-beam interferometer, Michelson and Morley looked for the speed difference between light travelling with and against Earth's motion through the supposed luminiferous aether. They found no difference — a null result that demolished the aether concept and directly motivated Special Relativity.
entries.note: Michelson, A. A. & Morley, E. W. (1887). 'On the Relative Motion of the Earth and the Luminiferous Ether.' American Journal of Science, 34, 333–345. Confirmed to parts in 10¹⁷ by modern laser interferometry (Herrmann, S. et al. 2009, Physical Review Letters, 103, 090401). Often called the most important null result in physics; it established that the speed of light is a fundamental constant independent of the observer's motion.

entries.label: Planck's Blackbody Law
entries.hint: Classical physics predicted that a hot object radiates infinite energy at short wavelengths — the 'ultraviolet catastrophe'. To fit the observed spectrum, Planck proposed that energy is emitted only in discrete packets of size hν. It was a mathematical fix he initially regarded as unphysical that turned out to be the founding act of quantum theory.
entries.note: Planck, M. (1900). 'Über eine Verbesserung der Wien'schen Spektralgleichung.' Verhandlungen der Deutschen Physikalischen Gesellschaft, 2, 202–204. Planck's law has been confirmed with extraordinary precision; the CMB spectrum measured by COBE (Mather et al. 1994, Astrophysical Journal Letters, Nobel 2006) is a perfect Planck blackbody to one part in 10⁴. Planck's constant h = 6.62607 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s now exactly defines the SI unit of mass.

entries.label: Einstein's Photoelectric Effect
entries.hint: Light ejects electrons from a metal surface only when its frequency exceeds a threshold — not when its intensity exceeds a threshold. Einstein explained this by proposing that light consists of discrete photons of energy hν; below the threshold frequency, no single photon has enough energy to liberate an electron regardless of how bright the light is.
entries.note: Einstein, A. (1905). 'Über einen die Erzeugung und Verwandlung des Lichtes betreffenden heuristischen Gesichtspunkt.' Annalen der Physik, 17, 132–148. Confirmed by Millikan, R. A. (1916). Physical Review, 7, 355–388 — Millikan initially hoped to disprove it. Einstein received the 1921 Nobel Prize for this paper, not for relativity. The photon concept was initially met with scepticism even after the experimental confirmation.

entries.label: Rutherford Scattering
entries.hint: Alpha particles fired at a thin gold foil were occasionally deflected through large angles — even backwards — which is impossible if atomic mass is spread evenly. Rutherford concluded that virtually all an atom's mass and positive charge is concentrated in a tiny central nucleus, founding nuclear physics.
entries.note: Geiger, H. & Marsden, E. (1909). 'On a Diffuse Reflection of the α-Particles.' Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 82, 495–500 (the observations). Rutherford, E. (1911). 'The Scattering of α and β Particles by Matter and the Structure of the Atom.' Philosophical Magazine, 21, 669–688 (the nuclear model). The nuclear atom remains the basis of all subsequent nuclear and particle physics; quantum mechanics later explained why the orbiting electrons don't spiral in.

entries.label: Bohr Hydrogen Model
entries.hint: Bohr quantised the electron's orbital angular momentum to derive the discrete energy levels of hydrogen, correctly predicting the wavelengths of every hydrogen spectral line. The model was a hybrid of classical orbits and quantum rules that was later superseded but established quantisation as physical reality rather than mathematical artifice.
entries.note: Bohr, N. (1913). 'On the Constitution of Atoms and Molecules.' Philosophical Magazine, 26, 1–25 (Part I of three). The model correctly predicts hydrogen spectra but fails for multi-electron atoms. Superseded by the Schrödinger equation (1926), which gives the hydrogen orbitals as probability distributions rather than definite orbits. The Bohr model's quantisation condition was later justified by de Broglie's matter-wave wavelength fitting the orbital circumference.

entries.label: Davisson-Germer Electron Diffraction
entries.loc: Bell Telephone Laboratories, Murray Hill, New Jersey
entries.hint: Slow electrons scattered from a nickel crystal produced diffraction rings identical to X-rays — the first direct experimental proof that electrons behave as waves. The wavelength matched de Broglie's prediction exactly, confirming that matter has wave properties and providing the empirical foundation for wave mechanics.
entries.note: Davisson, C. J. & Germer, L. H. (1927). 'Diffraction of Electrons by a Crystal of Nickel.' Physical Review, 30, 705–740. G. P. Thomson independently confirmed electron diffraction the same year using thin films. Nobel Prize 1937 (Davisson and Thomson — father J. J. Thomson won the Nobel for discovering the electron as a particle; son G. P. Thomson for demonstrating it is a wave). De Broglie received the 1929 Nobel for the theoretical prediction.

entries.label: Chadwick's Neutron Discovery
entries.hint: Chadwick identified a penetrating, uncharged radiation from beryllium as a new particle with roughly the same mass as a proton — the neutron. Its existence completed the proton-neutron model of the nucleus and provided a neutral probe that could initiate uranium fission without being repelled by the nucleus's positive charge.
entries.note: Chadwick, J. (1932). 'Possible Existence of a Neutron.' Nature, 129, 312. Extended in Chadwick, J. (1932). 'The Existence of a Neutron.' Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 136, 692–708. Nobel Prize in Physics 1935. The neutron's mass is 1.67493 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, slightly greater than the proton; a free neutron decays to a proton, electron and antineutrino with a half-life of ~879 seconds.

entries.label: Hahn & Strassmann Nuclear Fission
entries.loc: Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Chemistry, Berlin, Germany
entries.hint: Hahn and Strassmann bombarded uranium with neutrons and identified barium among the products — an element far too light to be a simple chipping off of uranium. Meitner and Frisch, working separately in Sweden, realised the nucleus had split in two, releasing energy consistent with Einstein's E=mc², opening the path to both nuclear power and nuclear weapons.
entries.note: Hahn, O. & Strassmann, F. (1939). 'Über den Nachweis und das Verhalten der bei der Bestrahlung des Urans mittels Neutronen entstehenden Erdalkalimetalle.' Die Naturwissenschaften, 27, 11–15. Meitner, L. & Frisch, O. R. (1939). 'Disintegration of Uranium by Neutrons.' Nature, 143, 239–240 (theoretical explanation). The 1944 Nobel Prize in Chemistry went to Hahn alone; Meitner's exclusion is widely regarded as the most unjust Nobel omission. Strassmann was also not awarded.

entries.label: Wu Parity Violation
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Chien-Shiung Wu cooled cobalt-60 nuclei to near absolute zero and aligned their spins with a magnetic field. Beta decay electrons were emitted preferentially in the direction opposite the nuclear spin — a result that is impossible if nature is mirror-symmetric. The weak force violates parity, overturning a conservation law that physicists had assumed to be fundamental.
entries.note: Wu, C. S., Ambler, E., Hayward, R. W., Hoppes, D. D. & Hudson, R. P. (1957). 'Experimental Test of Parity Conservation in Beta Decay.' Physical Review, 105, 1413–1415. Confirmed the theoretical prediction of Lee, T. D. & Yang, C. N. (1956) Physical Review, 104, 254, who received the 1957 Nobel Prize within the year. Wu did not receive the Nobel Prize; her exclusion is widely regarded as unjust.

entries.label: Pound-Rebka Gravitational Redshift
entries.loc: Jefferson Laboratory, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts
entries.hint: Using the Mössbauer effect to achieve the necessary frequency precision, Pound and Rebka measured the tiny frequency shift of gamma rays climbing 22.6 m against Earth's gravity — the first laboratory test of General Relativity's prediction that time runs slower in stronger gravitational fields.
entries.note: Pound, R. V. & Rebka, G. A. (1959). 'Gravitational Red-Shift in Nuclear Resonance.' Physical Review Letters, 3, 439–441. Agreed with GR to within 10%; remeasured by Pound & Snider (1964) Physical Review, 140, B788 to within 1%. Gravitational time dilation at centimetre scales later confirmed by Chou, C. W. et al. (2010). 'Optical Clocks and Relativity.' Science, 329, 1630–1633. The GPS system applies the same correction continuously.

entries.label: Aspect Bell Test
entries.hint: Alain Aspect generated pairs of entangled photons and measured their polarisations through randomly switched analysers, closing the major loophole in earlier Bell tests. The correlations exceeded the limit imposed by any local hidden-variable theory, demonstrating that quantum entanglement is real and nonlocal in a sense incompatible with classical realism — work recognised by the 2022 Nobel Prize in Physics, awarded jointly to Aspect, John Clauser, and Anton Zeilinger.
entries.note: Aspect, A., Dalibard, J. & Roger, G. (1982). 'Experimental Test of Bell's Inequalities Using Time-Varying Analyzers.' Physical Review Letters, 49, 1804–1807. Loophole-free Bell tests: Hensen, B. et al. (2015). Nature, 526, 682–686; Giustina, M. et al. (2015). Physical Review Letters, 115, 250401. Nobel Prize in Physics 2022 (Aspect, Clauser, Zeilinger). The violation rules out all local hidden-variable theories and has been exploited in quantum key distribution protocols.

entries.label: LIGO Gravitational Wave Detection
entries.hint: A chirp signal lasting 0.2 seconds was detected simultaneously at LIGO's Hanford and Livingston observatories — produced by two black holes of roughly 30 solar masses each spiralling together 1.3 billion light-years away. It was the first direct detection of gravitational waves, confirming the last major unverified prediction of General Relativity.
entries.note: Abbott, B. P. et al. (LIGO Scientific Collaboration & Virgo Collaboration) (2016). 'Observation of Gravitational Waves from a Binary Black Hole Merger.' Physical Review Letters, 116, 061102. Nobel Prize in Physics 2017 (Weiss, Barish, Thorne). The strain amplitude was Δl/l ≈ 10⁻²¹ — a displacement smaller than 1/1000 the diameter of a proton across a 4 km arm. As of 2023 the network has detected over 90 compact-object merger events.

entries.label: Penzias & Wilson CMB Discovery
entries.loc: Bell Telephone Laboratories, Holmdel, New Jersey
entries.hint: Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson, attempting to calibrate a large horn antenna for satellite communications, found a persistent 3.5 K microwave hiss that was uniform across the sky at all times of day and could not be attributed to equipment noise. This was the cosmic microwave background — the cooled afterglow of the hot early universe, first predicted by Alpher and Herman in 1948. The accidental discovery transformed cosmology from a largely theoretical enterprise into a precision observational science.
entries.note: Penzias, A. A. & Wilson, R. W. (1965). ‘A Measurement of Excess Antenna Temperature at 4080 Mc/s.’ Astrophysical Journal Letters, 142, 419–421. Nobel Prize in Physics 1978 (Penzias & Wilson). The CMB temperature is 2.7255 ± 0.0006 K; its anisotropies at 1 part in 100,000 encode the seeds of all large-scale structure. COBE (Mather et al. 1994; Nobel 2006), WMAP and Planck have mapped these fluctuations with increasing angular resolution. The CMB spectrum is the most nearly perfect blackbody ever measured.

entries.label: Bose-Einstein Condensate (Cornell & Wieman)
entries.loc: JILA, University of Colorado, Boulder, Colorado
entries.hint: Cornell and Wieman cooled a dilute gas of rubidium-87 atoms to 170 nanokelvin — colder than interstellar space — causing the atoms to collapse into the same quantum ground state and form a Bose-Einstein condensate: a new state of matter in which the entire sample behaves as a single macroscopic quantum wavefunction. The condensate realised a prediction made by Bose and Einstein in 1924 and provided the first direct bridge between quantum mechanics at the atomic scale and observable macroscopic quantum behaviour.
entries.note: Anderson, M. H., Ensher, J. R., Matthews, M. R., Wieman, C. E. & Cornell, E. A. (1995). ‘Observation of Bose-Einstein Condensation in a Dilute Atomic Vapor.’ Science, 269, 198–201. Nobel Prize in Physics 2001 (Cornell, Wieman, and Ketterle, who independently produced a BEC at MIT). BECs now serve as quantum simulators for superfluidity and topological phases; atom interferometry using BECs achieves gravitational acceleration measurements at parts in 10¹⁰.

entries.label: Neutrino Oscillations & Mass (Super-K/SNO)
entries.hint: Super-Kamiokande (1998) found that atmospheric muon neutrinos disappear as they travel through the Earth — a phenomenon called neutrino oscillation that is only possible if neutrinos have non-zero mass. SNO (2001) confirmed that solar neutrinos oscillate between all three flavours en route from the Sun. Because the original Standard Model assumes massless neutrinos, these results are the clearest current experimental proof of physics beyond the Standard Model.
entries.note: Super-Kamiokande: Fukuda, Y. et al. (1998). ‘Evidence for an Oscillatory Signature in Atmospheric Neutrino Oscillation.’ Physical Review Letters, 81, 1562–1567. SNO: Ahmad, Q. R. et al. (2001). Physical Review Letters, 87, 071301. Nobel Prize in Physics 2015 (Kajita for Super-K; McDonald for SNO). Neutrino masses are sub-eV but the absolute mass scale and mass ordering (normal vs inverted hierarchy) remain unknown. Neutrino mass is the most definitively established extension of the Standard Model particle content.

entries.label: Higgs Boson Discovery (ATLAS & CMS)
entries.hint: Two independent detector collaborations — ATLAS and CMS — simultaneously announced the observation of a new boson at 125 GeV consistent with the long-sought Higgs particle, completing the Standard Model’s particle content after a 48-year search. The Higgs field gives mass to the W and Z bosons and, via Yukawa couplings, to quarks and charged leptons; the Higgs boson is the quantised excitation of that field. The discovery required collisions at 8 TeV, several petabytes of data, and roughly 10,000 physicists across two collaborations.
entries.note: ATLAS Collaboration (2012). ‘Observation of a new boson at a mass of 125 GeV with the ATLAS detector at the LHC.’ Physics Letters B, 716, 1–29. CMS Collaboration (2012). Physics Letters B, 716, 30–61. Nobel Prize in Physics 2013 (Higgs and Englert). The boson’s spin-0, CP-even character and coupling strengths are all consistent with the Standard Model Higgs to within current precision. Deviations in Higgs couplings remain the most sensitive probe of beyond-Standard-Model physics at the LHC.

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named, replicable phenomena in quantum, nuclear and relativistic physics

entries.label: Quantum Tunnelling
entries.hint: A quantum particle can penetrate a potential energy barrier it classically lacks the energy to cross — because the wavefunction is nonzero inside the barrier, giving a finite probability of emerging on the other side. Tunnelling explains alpha decay, enables nuclear fusion inside stars at temperatures far below the classical threshold, and is exploited in tunnel diodes and the scanning tunnelling microscope.
entries.note: Gamow, G. (1928). 'Zur Quantentheorie des Atomkernes.' Zeitschrift für Physik, 51, 204–212. Gurney, R. W. & Condon, E. U. (1929) independently explained alpha decay by tunnelling. The tunnel diode (Esaki 1958, Nobel 1973) and scanning tunnelling microscope (Binnig & Rohrer 1981, Nobel 1986) are direct applications; STM achieves atomic-scale surface imaging by measuring tunnelling current as a function of tip position.

entries.label: Compton Scattering
entries.hint: When X-rays scatter off electrons, their wavelength increases by an amount that depends only on the scattering angle — not on the wavelength of the incident light. Compton showed this follows directly from treating the photon as a particle with momentum hν/c that collides elastically with the electron, providing definitive proof that photons carry momentum.
entries.note: Compton, A. H. (1923). 'A Quantum Theory of the Scattering of X-rays by Light Elements.' Physical Review, 21, 483–502. Nobel Prize in Physics 1927. The Compton wavelength shift Δλ = (h/mₑc)(1−cosθ) = 0.00243(1−cosθ) nm depends only on scattering angle. The effect is distinct from the photoelectric effect: Compton scattering involves partial energy transfer (the photon survives); the photoelectric effect involves complete absorption.

entries.label: Lamb Shift
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Dirac's equation predicted that two hydrogen energy levels (2S½ and 2P½) should be exactly degenerate. Lamb and Retherford measured a tiny splitting between them using microwave techniques. This splitting — caused by fluctuations of the quantum vacuum — could not be explained without QED and became the key motivation for developing renormalisation.
entries.note: Lamb, W. E. & Retherford, R. C. (1947). 'Fine Structure of the Hydrogen Atom by a Microwave Method.' Physical Review, 72, 241–243. Nobel Prize in Physics 1955 (to Lamb). The shift was first computed by Bethe, H. A. (1947) Physical Review, 72, 339, using a non-relativistic renormalisation. Feynman and Schwinger subsequently derived the full QED result. The measured Lamb shift (1057.8 MHz) agrees with QED to better than 1 part per million.

entries.label: Radioactive Decay & Half-Life
entries.hint: Radioactive elements spontaneously transmute into other elements at a constant fractional rate — the probability per unit time of any given nucleus decaying is fixed. This gives exponential decay characterised by a half-life, and means atoms are not immutable. The exponential decay law follows directly from quantum mechanical transition probabilities.
entries.note: Rutherford, E. & Soddy, F. (1902). 'The Cause and Nature of Radioactivity.' Philosophical Magazine, 4, 370–396 & 569–585. The exponential decay law emerges from a constant transition probability per unit time — a consequence of quantum mechanics. Half-lives span 26 orders of magnitude: polonium-212 (0.3 μs) to tellurium-128 (2.2 × 10²⁴ years). Nobel Prize to Rutherford in Chemistry 1908; to Soddy in Chemistry 1921.

entries.label: Nuclear Binding Energy & Mass Defect
entries.hint: A nucleus weighs slightly less than the sum of its constituent protons and neutrons — the missing mass is the binding energy holding it together (via E=mc²). The binding energy per nucleon peaks at iron-56, which means fission releases energy for heavy nuclei and fusion releases energy for light ones, explaining both stellar energy generation and nuclear weapons.
entries.note: Mass defect first measured by Aston, F. W. (1920) using mass spectrometry; Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1922. Semi-empirical mass formula (Bethe-Weizsäcker formula): von Weizsäcker, C. F. (1935). Zeitschrift für Physik, 96, 431. The iron-56 binding energy peak (8.79 MeV/nucleon) is why supernova iron cores collapse; endothermic fusion beyond iron triggers core collapse. Nuclear binding energies are measured to 10 keV precision using Penning traps.

entries.label: Gravitational Time Dilation
entries.loc: Jefferson Laboratory, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts
entries.hint: Clocks run slower in stronger gravitational fields — a direct consequence of General Relativity. GPS satellites experience both Special Relativistic time dilation (their speed slows their clocks by 7 μs/day) and gravitational time dilation (weaker gravity speeds their clocks by 45 μs/day), requiring a net correction of +38 μs/day or position errors would accumulate at ~10 km/day.
entries.note: First confirmed: Pound, R. V. & Rebka, G. A. (1959). Physical Review Letters, 3, 439. Confirmed at centimetre scales: Chou, C. W. et al. (2010). Science, 329, 1630 using optical atomic clocks. Gravitational time dilation has also been confirmed by the Hafele-Keating experiment (1971, Science, 177, 166) flying atomic clocks on aircraft. GPS relativistic corrections: Ashby, N. (2003). Living Reviews in Relativity, 6, 1.

entries.label: Gravitational Lensing
entries.hint: Light bends as it passes a massive object because spacetime is curved — Eddington's 1919 solar eclipse expedition measured the apparent displacement of stars near the sun's limb and confirmed General Relativity's prediction, which is twice the Newtonian value. Gravitational lensing is now a precision cosmological tool for mapping dark matter and measuring the Hubble constant.
entries.note: Dyson, F. W., Eddington, A. S. & Davidson, C. (1920). 'A Determination of the Deflection of Light by the Sun's Gravitational Field.' Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, 220, 291–333. Eddington's 1919 measurement had large uncertainties and the analysis was imperfect; confirmed to better than 0.1% by radio-interferometry (Shapiro, I. I. et al. 2004, Physical Review Letters, 92, 121101). Strong gravitational lensing of distant galaxies is now used to constrain dark matter distribution (Clowe et al. 2006, Astrophysical Journal Letters, 648, L109).

entries.label: Bell Inequality Violation
entries.hint: Bell's theorem proved that any local hidden-variable theory must satisfy certain statistical inequalities on the correlations between distant measurements. Quantum entangled particle pairs violate these inequalities — a result that rules out local realism as an explanation of quantum correlations and implies that nature is fundamentally nonlocal in the quantum sense.
entries.note: Bell, J. S. (1964). 'On the Einstein Podolsky Rosen Paradox.' Physics, 1, 195–200 (theoretical). Experimental violation: Aspect, A. et al. (1982). Physical Review Letters, 49, 1804. Loophole-free: Hensen, B. et al. (2015). Nature, 526, 682 (detection loophole and locality loophole both closed simultaneously). Nobel Prize 2022. The violation has been used to certify quantum cryptography keys and to generate certified randomness from quantum devices.

entries.label: Mass-Energy Equivalence E=mc²
entries.hint: A direct consequence of Special Relativity: mass and energy are interconvertible, with the conversion factor c² ≈ 9 × 10¹⁶ J/kg. A tiny amount of mass converts to an enormous quantity of energy, explaining why the sun shines (fusion converts ~0.7% of hydrogen mass to energy), why nuclear fission releases megajoules per gram, and why matter-antimatter annihilation is perfectly efficient.
entries.note: Einstein, A. (1905). 'Ist die Trägheit eines Körpers von seinem Energieinhalt abhängig?' Annalen der Physik, 18, 639–641. The relation is exact and applies to rest mass and rest energy; the full relativistic expression is E² = (pc)² + (m₀c²)². Directly confirmed by nuclear mass measurements (Aston 1920) and by the energy yield of fission reactions (St8). The mass-energy equivalence underpins every nuclear energy calculation.

categories.label: Key Concepts
categories.subtitle: The building blocks and vocabulary every student must command

entries.label: Wave-Particle Duality
entries.hint: Quantum objects exhibit wave properties (interference, diffraction) or particle properties (discrete impacts, definite detection events) depending on the experimental context — neither description is complete alone. de Broglie proposed that all matter has a wavelength λ = h/p; Davisson and Germer confirmed this for electrons; the double-slit experiment with individual electrons or photons makes the duality vivid.
entries.note: de Broglie, L. (1924). 'Recherches sur la théorie des quanta.' Annales de Physique, 3, 22–128. Nobel Prize 1929. Born's probability interpretation (1926) gave the wavefunction its physical meaning: the wave is a probability amplitude, the particle is what is detected. Duality is not a paradox but a feature of quantum formalism; Bohr called the two aspects 'complementary'. It is exploited in electron microscopy (resolution λ ≈ pm for 100 keV electrons).

entries.label: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
entries.hint: Conjugate variables such as position and momentum, or energy and time, cannot both be precisely specified simultaneously: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2. This is not about measurement disturbance but about the impossibility of preparing a quantum state with definite values for both — a fundamental feature of waves. It sets the minimum size of atomic orbitals and makes the zero-point energy of quantum systems nonzero.
entries.note: Heisenberg, W. (1927). 'Über den anschaulichen Inhalt der quantentheoretischen Kinematik und Mechanik.' Zeitschrift für Physik, 43, 172–198. Generalised by Robertson, H. P. (1929) Physical Review, 34, 163 to all conjugate pairs. The popular 'observer disturbs the system' explanation is misleading: the Robertson derivation involves no measurement at all. Time-energy uncertainty (ΔEΔt ≥ ℏ/2) underlies the natural linewidth of atomic transitions and the virtual particle picture of QFT.

entries.label: Quantum Superposition & Entanglement
entries.hint: Quantum systems can exist in a superposition of multiple distinct states simultaneously; when two particles interact, their wavefunctions can become entangled — measuring one particle instantly determines the correlated outcome of measuring the other, regardless of the distance between them. These resources are now exploited in quantum computing, quantum cryptography, and quantum teleportation.
entries.note: Superposition follows from the linearity of the Schrödinger equation. Entanglement named and analysed by Schrödinger, E. (1935). 'Discussion of Probability Relations between Separated Systems.' Mathematical Proceedings of the Cambridge Philosophical Society, 31, 555–563. EPR paradox: Einstein, A., Podolsky, B. & Rosen, N. (1935). Physical Review, 47, 777. Quantum computing exploits superposition and entanglement: Shor's factoring algorithm (1994) and Grover's search algorithm (1996). Quantum teleportation: Bennett, C. H. et al. (1993). Physical Review Letters, 70, 1895.

entries.label: Nuclear Fission vs Fusion
entries.hint: Fission splits a heavy nucleus (uranium-235, plutonium-239) releasing ~200 MeV per event by moving down the binding energy curve from a less-stable heavy nucleus to more-stable mid-weight products. Fusion joins light nuclei (deuterium, tritium) releasing even more energy per kilogram — but requires extreme temperatures (~100 million K) to overcome electrostatic repulsion, making controlled fusion the grand engineering challenge of the 21st century.
entries.note: Systematic comparison of fission and fusion energetics follows from the nuclear binding energy curve (Aston 1920; Bethe-Weizsäcker formula 1935). Stellar fusion: Bethe, H. A. (1939). 'Energy Production in Stars.' Physical Review, 55, 434–456 (Nobel 1967). Controlled fusion: ITER project (target: 500 MW from 50 MW input). NIF achieved ignition in December 2022 (Abu-Shawareb, O. A. et al. 2024, Physical Review Letters, 132, 065102) — net energy gain from the laser capsule, not the full driver system.

entries.label: Antimatter
entries.hint: Dirac's relativistic quantum equation for the electron had two solutions — one for the electron and one for a particle of equal mass but opposite charge. Anderson discovered this positron in cosmic rays in 1932. Every fundamental particle has an antiparticle; particle-antiparticle annihilation converts mass entirely to energy. The matter-antimatter asymmetry of the universe — why matter survived — is one of the deepest unsolved problems in physics.
entries.note: Dirac, P. A. M. (1928). 'The Quantum Theory of the Electron.' Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 117, 610–624. Anderson, C. D. (1933). 'The Positive Electron.' Physical Review, 43, 491–494 (Nobel 1936). CP violation (matter-antimatter asymmetry): Christenson, J. H. et al. (1964). Physical Review Letters, 13, 138. Current LHCb measurements continue to constrain baryogenesis models (LHCb Collaboration, 2019, Nature Physics, 15, 917). The Standard Model's CP violation is insufficient to explain the observed matter dominance.

entries.label: Spacetime & Curvature
entries.hint: Space and time are not separate but unified into a four-dimensional manifold whose geometry is curved by mass and energy. Free-falling objects follow geodesics — the straightest possible paths in curved spacetime — and what we call gravity is the geometry of this manifold. The curvature of spacetime also slows time, bends light, and generates gravitational waves.
entries.note: Minkowski, H. (1908). 'Raum und Zeit.' Physikalische Zeitschrift, 10, 104–111 — unified space and time for Special Relativity. The full Riemannian geometry of curved spacetime was applied by Einstein (1915). The geodesic deviation equation (Jacobi field) quantifies how curvature causes initially parallel free-fall trajectories to converge or diverge — the tidal forces of gravity. Spacetime curvature is measured via metric tensor components gᵤᵥ satisfying the Einstein field equations Gᵤᵥ = 8πGTᵤᵥ/c⁴.

entries.label: Quantisation
entries.hint: Physical quantities that are continuous in classical physics — energy, angular momentum, charge, magnetic flux — take only discrete (quantised) values at the atomic scale. Planck's energy quanta (1900), Bohr's orbital shells (1913), and the nuclear shell model (1949) are successive applications of the same principle; quantum field theory extends it to fields themselves, so even the electromagnetic field is made of discrete photons.
entries.note: Planck's energy quantisation (1900) was the first instance. Angular momentum quantisation: Bohr (1913). Magnetic flux quantisation in superconductors: Deaver & Fairbank (1961) Physical Review Letters, 7, 43. Charge quantisation (quarks have e/3, 2e/3): confirmed by SLAC deep inelastic scattering (1968). Second quantisation (Dirac 1927) formalised the quantisation of fields — photons, phonons and all bosonic excitations emerge as quanta of their respective quantum fields.

categories.label: Methods & Approaches
categories.subtitle: The tools and paradigms that generate the field's evidence

entries.label: Particle Detectors
entries.hint: From Wilson's cloud chamber (1911) to bubble chambers, Geiger-Müller counters, and modern silicon pixel trackers, particle detectors make subatomic interactions legible by recording the tracks, energies and momenta of particles. Each generation enabled a new layer of discovery — the positron, muon, and kaon all appeared first in detector records.
entries.note: Wilson, C. T. R. (1911). 'On a Method of Making Visible the Paths of Ionising Particles.' Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 85, 285–288 (Nobel 1927). Bubble chamber: Glaser, D. A. (1952) Physical Review, 87, 665 (Nobel 1960). Silicon pixel detectors (1980s): now standard at LHC (CMS inner tracker: 75 million silicon pixels). Each LHC proton-proton collision generates ~1 MB of raw data; real-time triggers filter ~40 MHz collision rate down to ~1 kHz for storage.

entries.label: Spectroscopy
entries.hint: Each element emits and absorbs light at a unique set of wavelengths — its spectral fingerprint. Kirchhoff and Bunsen established spectroscopy as a tool of chemical analysis; Bohr's model of the atom explained why spectral lines exist; quantum mechanics gave exact predictions for every line. Spectroscopy remains the primary tool for probing atomic structure, from laboratory plasmas to the spectra of distant galaxies.
entries.note: Kirchhoff, G. R. & Bunsen, R. W. (1860). 'Chemische Analyse durch Spectralbeobachtungen.' Annalen der Physik und Chemie, 110, 161–189. Modern spectroscopy spans 25 orders of magnitude in frequency, from radio (NMR) to gamma-ray. NMR spectroscopy (Bloch & Purcell 1946, Nobel 1952) determines molecular structure; mass spectrometry (Aston, Nobel 1922) measures atomic masses; Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) is standard for molecular identification.

entries.label: Particle Accelerators & Colliders
entries.hint: Lawrence's cyclotron (1932) accelerated ions using oscillating electric fields within a magnetic spiral. Subsequent synchrotrons, linear accelerators, and storage-ring colliders have reached TeV energies, enabling the direct production and study of quarks, W and Z bosons, and the Higgs. Every major discovery in particle physics since 1950 has depended on accelerator technology.
entries.note: Lawrence, E. O. (1932). 'The Production of High Speed Light Ions Without the Use of High Voltages.' Physical Review, 40, 19–35 (Nobel 1939). CERN LHC (operational 2008): 27 km ring, protons at 6.8 TeV per beam; discovered the Higgs boson in 2012. Stored beam energy: ~360 MJ per beam — equivalent to a 200 tonne aircraft at 300 km/h. The proposed Future Circular Collider (FCC) would be 100 km in circumference with 100 TeV centre-of-mass energy.

entries.label: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)
entries.hint: Atomic nuclei with non-zero spin absorb and re-emit radiofrequency radiation at a resonant frequency determined by the local magnetic environment — giving a distinctive signal for each chemical group. NMR spectroscopy determines the three-dimensional structure of molecules in solution; Lauterbur's addition of field gradients turned NMR into MRI.
entries.note: Purcell, E. M., Torrey, H. C. & Pound, R. V. (1946). 'Resonance Absorption by Nuclear Magnetic Moments in a Solid.' Physical Review, 69, 37. Bloch, F. et al. (1946). 'Nuclear Induction.' Physical Review, 70, 460. Nobel Prize 1952 (Bloch & Purcell). Two-dimensional NMR (Ernst 1991 Nobel) enabled full protein structure determination. Modern cryo-EM (Nobel 2017) complements NMR for very large molecular complexes.

entries.label: Radiocarbon Dating
entries.hint: Atmospheric carbon-14 is incorporated into living tissue and begins decaying (half-life 5,730 years) after death, allowing organic materials to be dated up to ~50,000 years old by measuring the remaining ¹⁴C fraction. Calibrated against tree rings and ice cores, it transformed archaeology, palaeoclimatology, and our understanding of human prehistory.
entries.note: Libby, W. F., Anderson, E. C. & Arnold, J. R. (1949). 'Age Determination by Radiocarbon Content: World-Wide Assay of Natural Radiocarbon.' Science, 109, 227–228. Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1960. Calibration curve (IntCal): updated every few years by the IntCal Working Group; IntCal20 (Reimer et al. 2020, Radiocarbon) uses 9,000 individual data points. Accelerator mass spectrometry (AMS) reduces sample requirements from grams to milligrams and extends dating range.

entries.label: Feynman Diagrams
entries.hint: Feynman's pictorial notation represents each term in the perturbative expansion of a quantum field theory — lines for propagating particles, vertices for interactions. Each diagram corresponds to a definite mathematical expression for a scattering amplitude, making systematic calculation of cross-sections and decay rates tractable and providing an intuitive picture of virtual particle exchange.
entries.note: Feynman, R. P. (1949). 'Space-Time Approach to Quantum Electrodynamics.' Physical Review, 76, 769–789. Dyson, F. J. (1949) Physical Review, 75, 486 proved Feynman's rules produce the same results as Schwinger and Tomonaga's operator methods. Feynman diagrams are used in QED, QCD, the electroweak theory and effective field theories. At high loop orders (5+ loops), computer algebra systems are needed; the electron g-2 calculation uses ~13,000 diagrams at four-loop order.

categories.label: Pioneers & Theorists
categories.subtitle: The researchers who shaped the discipline

entries.label: Marie Curie
entries.loc: École de Physique et de Chimie, Paris, France
entries.hint: Curie coined the term 'radioactivity', discovered polonium and radium, and was the first person to win two Nobel Prizes. Her systematic measurement of radioactive emissions as an intrinsic atomic property overturned the view that atoms are immutable, and her purification of radium laid the groundwork for nuclear medicine and radiation therapy.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1903 (with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel). Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1911 (solo, for discovery of polonium and radium). Key papers: Curie, M. & Curie, P. (1898). Comptes Rendus, 127, 1215–1217 (polonium); 1215–1217 & 127, 175 (radium). Curie was denied membership in the French Académie des Sciences in 1911 despite her two Nobel Prizes; the Académie did not admit women until 1962. Her working notebooks remain radioactive and are stored in lead-lined boxes.

entries.label: Max Planck
entries.hint: Planck introduced the quantum of action h in 1900 to explain blackbody radiation — an act he initially considered a mathematical device rather than a physical reality. He spent the following years trying to derive the quantum hypothesis from classical physics, and only reluctantly accepted that it represented a fundamental break. He is the reluctant revolutionary at the origin of quantum theory.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1918. Key paper: Planck, M. (1900). 'Zur Theorie des Gesetzes der Energieverteilung im Normalspektrum.' Verhandlungen der Deutschen Physikalischen Gesellschaft, 2, 237–245. Planck's constant h = 6.62607 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s now exactly defines the SI kilogram. Planck remained in Germany during the Nazi period; his son Erwin was executed for involvement in the 1944 assassination attempt on Hitler. Planck lobbied on behalf of Jewish colleagues, including Lisa Meitner.

entries.label: Albert Einstein
entries.hint: Einstein's 1905 annus mirabilis produced four papers that each founded a field: the photoelectric effect (quantum of light), Brownian motion (proof of atoms), Special Relativity, and mass-energy equivalence. His 1915 General Relativity remains the most elegant physical theory ever constructed — and his lifelong opposition to Copenhagen quantum mechanics kept the foundations debate alive.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1921 (photoelectric effect, not relativity — the committee was still uncertain about relativity). Key papers: Annalen der Physik, 17 & 18, 1905; Annalen der Physik, 49, 1916. EPR paper: Einstein, A., Podolsky, B. & Rosen, N. (1935). Physical Review, 47, 777 — argued quantum mechanics is incomplete; this paper ultimately led to Bell's theorem and quantum information theory. Einstein held the first permanent faculty position at the Institute for Advanced Study, Princeton (from 1933).

entries.label: Ernest Rutherford
entries.hint: Rutherford discovered the nuclear atom (1911), demonstrated radioactive transmutation of elements (with Soddy, 1902), and first split the atom artificially by bombarding nitrogen with alpha particles (1917). His Cavendish Laboratory as director produced more Nobel laureates in physics than any other institution in the early 20th century.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1908. Key papers: Rutherford & Soddy (1902) Philosophical Magazine, 4, 370; Geiger & Marsden (1909) Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 82, 495; Rutherford (1911) Philosophical Magazine, 21, 669. Rutherford famously told a reporter: 'All science is either physics or stamp collecting.' Students and associates who won Nobel Prizes include Bohr, Chadwick, Cockcroft, Walton, Blackett, and Kapitza.

entries.label: Niels Bohr
entries.hint: Bohr quantised the hydrogen atom in 1913 and later articulated the Copenhagen Interpretation — his Institute for Theoretical Physics became the world centre of quantum mechanics in the 1920s and 1930s, attracting Heisenberg, Pauli, Dirac, and Gamow. His concept of complementarity held that wave and particle pictures are mutually exclusive but jointly necessary.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1922. Key papers: Bohr, N. (1913). Philosophical Magazine, 26, 1–25 (hydrogen model). Complementarity: Bohr, N. (1928). Nature, 121, 580. Bohr escaped from Denmark in 1943 on a fishing boat after the German occupation, eventually contributing to the Manhattan Project at Los Alamos under the alias 'Nicholas Baker'. His Institute (now the Niels Bohr Institute) remains one of the leading theoretical physics centres in Europe.

entries.label: Werner Heisenberg
entries.hint: Heisenberg created matrix mechanics in 1925 and derived the uncertainty principle in 1927 — the two cornerstones of quantum mechanics as a complete theory. He led the German nuclear programme (Uranverein) during World War II; whether he deliberately obstructed its progress or simply failed to achieve a working reactor remains a contested historical question.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1932. Key papers: Heisenberg (1925) Zeitschrift für Physik, 33, 879; (1927) Zeitschrift für Physik, 43, 172. Farm Hall transcripts (1945, declassified 1992): Heisenberg and nine other German physicists were secretly recorded after hearing of Hiroshima — the transcripts show Heisenberg initially calculated the critical mass incorrectly. Analysis: Rose, P. L. (1998) Heisenberg and the Nazi Atomic Bomb Project. University of California Press.

entries.label: Erwin Schrödinger
entries.hint: Schrödinger formulated wave mechanics in 1926, giving the wavefunction equation that became the workhorse of quantum chemistry and condensed matter physics — and was deeply uncomfortable with the probabilistic Born interpretation of his own equation. His 1935 cat thought experiment remains the canonical statement of the measurement problem.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1933 (shared with Dirac). Key papers: Schrödinger (1926) Annalen der Physik, 79, 361 (four-part series). Cat paper: Schrödinger, E. (1935). 'Die gegenwärtige Situation in der Quantenmechanik.' Naturwissenschaften, 23, 807–812. Schrödinger (1944). What is Life? (Cambridge University Press) — influence on Crick and Watson's pursuit of the structure of DNA is documented in Watson, J. D. (1968). The Double Helix.

entries.label: Paul Dirac
entries.hint: Dirac unified Special Relativity with quantum mechanics in the Dirac equation (1928), predicting that the electron has an antiparticle before it was discovered. He developed the foundations of quantum field theory, invented the bra-ket notation, and is widely regarded as the most mathematically precise physicist of the 20th century — combining abstract beauty with physical reality.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1933 (shared with Schrödinger). Key papers: Dirac, P. A. M. (1928). Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 117, 610 (relativistic electron equation); (1931) Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 133, 60 (magnetic monopole and positron prediction). Dirac was notoriously taciturn; the 'Dirac' (a unit of one word per hour) was reportedly coined by Bohr. His Principles of Quantum Mechanics (1930, Oxford) remains a foundational text.

entries.label: James Chadwick
entries.hint: Chadwick's discovery of the neutron in 1932 completed the proton-neutron model of the nucleus and provided the neutral projectile that made nuclear fission possible. He later led the British contingent of the Manhattan Project at Los Alamos, reportedly the only person Oppenheimer trusted to adjudicate technical disputes between American and British scientists.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1935. Key papers: Chadwick, J. (1932). Nature, 129, 312; (1932). Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 136, 692. Chadwick was interned in Germany during World War I (as a student in Geiger's lab when war broke out) and witnessed his mentor Rutherford's death in 1937. His role in the Manhattan Project is documented in Brown, A. (1997). The Neutron and the Bomb. Oxford University Press.

entries.label: Lise Meitner
entries.hint: Meitner co-led the Berlin nuclear physics programme with Otto Hahn for 30 years, co-discovered protactinium, and — working in exile with her nephew Otto Robert Frisch, who coined the term “fission” — provided the theoretical explanation of nuclear fission using the liquid-drop model and E=mc². Her exclusion from the 1944 Nobel Prize given to Hahn alone is widely regarded as the most unjust omission in Nobel history.
entries.note: Key paper: Meitner, L. & Frisch, O. R. (1939). 'Disintegration of Uranium by Neutrons: a New Type of Nuclear Reaction.' Nature, 143, 239–240. Meitner was nominated 48 times for the Nobel Prize and never awarded it. She refused to participate in the Manhattan Project, reportedly saying 'I will have nothing to do with a bomb.' Element 109, Meitnerium (Mt), was named in her honour in 1997. Ruth Lewin Sime (1996). Lise Meitner: A Life in Physics. University of California Press.

entries.label: Enrico Fermi
entries.hint: Fermi developed the quantum statistical mechanics of fermions (Fermi-Dirac statistics, 1926), the first quantitative theory of beta decay (1934), and built the first nuclear reactor (Chicago Pile-1, 1942). He was equally brilliant as theorist and experimentalist — a combination so rare that his colleagues called it 'the Fermi touch'.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1938 (for neutron bombardment experiments in Rome). Key papers: Fermi, E. (1934). 'Versuch einer Theorie der β-Strahlen.' Zeitschrift für Physik, 88, 161–177 (beta decay). Chicago Pile-1 first chain reaction: 2 December 1942. Fermi fled Fascist Italy in 1938, going directly from the Nobel ceremony in Stockholm to the United States. The Fermi paradox ('Where is everybody?') was coined at a Los Alamos lunch discussion in 1950.

entries.label: Richard Feynman
entries.hint: Feynman developed the path-integral formulation of quantum mechanics, a key renormalisation procedure for QED, and the Feynman diagram notation — becoming physics' most effective communicator in the process. His Lectures on Physics (1964) remain the canonical physics education text; his 1986 diagnosis of the Challenger O-ring failure exemplified the same physical intuition.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physics 1965 (shared with Schwinger and Tomonaga). Key papers: Feynman, R. P. (1948). Physical Review, 74, 939 (path integral); (1949) Physical Review, 76, 769 (QED). The Feynman Lectures on Physics (Feynman, Leighton & Sands, 1964, Addison-Wesley) have sold over 1.5 million copies and are available freely online. Feynman's bongo drumming and his bongo drum story, along with the O-ring demonstration, remain cultural touchstones of scientific communication.

entries.label: Vera Rubin
entries.loc: Carnegie Institution of Washington, Washington, D.C.
entries.hint: Rubin’s systematic spectroscopic measurements of spiral galaxy rotation curves through the 1970s demonstrated that stellar orbital speeds remain nearly constant far from the galactic centre — impossible given the visible mass alone. Her work transformed dark matter from Zwicky’s contested inference into a widely accepted observational fact, and established the rotation curve technique as the primary evidence for dark matter in galaxies.
entries.note: Rubin, V. C. & Ford, W. K. (1970). ‘Rotation of the Andromeda Nebula from a Spectroscopic Survey of Emission Regions.’ Astrophysical Journal, 159, 379–403. Rubin, V. C., Thonnard, N. & Ford, W. K. (1980). Astrophysical Journal, 238, 471. Rubin was the first woman permitted to observe at Palomar Observatory (1965) and one of the first women elected to the US National Academy of Sciences astronomy section. She was nominated for the Nobel Prize but died in December 2016 before it was awarded for dark matter evidence.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Live controversies — where the field is actively contesting the evidence

entries.label: Copenhagen vs Hidden Variables
entries.hint: The Bohr-Einstein debates set the terms: is the wavefunction a complete description of reality (Copenhagen), or does it mask deeper deterministic hidden variables (Einstein, Bell, de Broglie)? Bell's theorem (1964) and subsequent experiments ruled out local hidden variables. Non-local hidden-variable theories (de Broglie-Bohm pilot waves) remain viable but have found no experimental signature beyond standard QM.
entries.note: The debates are documented in Bohr, N. (1949). In P. A. Schilpp (Ed.) Albert Einstein: Philosopher-Scientist. Library of Living Philosophers. Bell, J. S. (1964). Physics, 1, 195–200. De Broglie-Bohm pilot wave: Bohm, D. (1952). Physical Review, 85, 166–179 & 180–193 — a working deterministic hidden-variable theory, fully nonlocal. Review: Goldstein, S. (2021). 'Bohmian Mechanics.' Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy. Loophole-free experiments (2015) rule out local hidden variables but not Bohmian mechanics.

entries.label: Many-Worlds Interpretation
entries.hint: Everett proposed that the wavefunction never collapses — instead, every measurement outcome occurs in a branching universal wavefunction, and observers find themselves in definite branches by virtue of decoherence. The interpretation avoids the measurement problem but requires an unimaginably large ontology, and deriving the Born probability rule from branching structure alone is mathematically contested.
entries.note: Everett, H. (1957). '"Relative State" Formulation of Quantum Mechanics.' Reviews of Modern Physics, 29, 454–462. Popularised by DeWitt, B. S. (1970) Physics Today. Deutsch, D. (1999). Proceedings of the Royal Society A, 455, 3129 — decision-theoretic derivation of the Born rule; contested by Albert (2010) and others. Carroll, S. (2019). Something Deeply Hidden. Dutton — modern defence. The 'quantum suicide' argument and 'quantum immortality' are popular philosophical extrapolations not endorsed by most physicists.

entries.label: Quantum Gravity: String Theory vs Loop QG
entries.hint: General Relativity and quantum mechanics are individually the most successful physical theories ever devised, but they are mutually incompatible at the Planck scale. String theory unifies all forces in extra dimensions; loop quantum gravity quantises spacetime itself into discrete chunks. Neither has produced an experimentally testable prediction accessible to current technology.
entries.note: Superstring revolution: Green, M. B. & Schwarz, J. H. (1984). Physics Letters B, 149, 117–122; Witten, E. (1995) announced M-theory unifying five string theories. Loop quantum gravity: Rovelli, C. & Smolin, L. (1988). Nuclear Physics B, 331, 80–152. Penrose, R. (2004). The Road to Reality. Jonathan Cape — critical survey of both programmes. Both approaches predict different phenomena near the Planck scale (10⁻³⁵ m, 10¹⁹ GeV) — currently ~15 orders of magnitude beyond LHC energies.

entries.label: Black Hole Information Paradox
entries.hint: Hawking showed black holes radiate thermally and eventually evaporate — but thermal radiation is featureless and carries no information about what fell in. If information is truly lost, quantum mechanical unitarity is violated. String theory suggests information is preserved on the horizon; recent holographic calculations suggest it is encoded in late-time Hawking radiation.
entries.note: Hawking, S. W. (1975). 'Particle Creation by Black Holes.' Communications in Mathematical Physics, 43, 199–220. Hawking temperature: T = ℏc³/(8πGMkB). Firewall argument: Almheiri, A., Marolf, D., Polchinski, J. & Sully, J. (2013). JHEP, 2013, 62. Island formula (holographic resolution): Penington, G. (2020). JHEP, 2020, 2; Almheiri, A. et al. (2020). JHEP, 2020, 13. Hawking recanted information loss at the 2004 Dublin GR conference but the consensus resolution remains unsettled.

entries.label: The Measurement Problem / Schrödinger's Cat
entries.hint: Quantum mechanics predicts that macroscopic systems can be in superpositions of distinct outcomes — Schrödinger's cat is both alive and dead until observed. The measurement problem asks what physical process singles out one outcome, and whether there is an objective physical collapse or merely the appearance of one. Decoherence explains why superpositions are unobservable in practice but does not select a single outcome.
entries.note: Schrödinger, E. (1935). 'Die gegenwärtige Situation in der Quantenmechanik.' Naturwissenschaften, 23, 807–812. Objective collapse: Ghirardi, G. C., Rimini, A. & Weber, T. (1986). Physical Review D, 34, 470 (GRW theory). Decoherence: Zeh, H. D. (1970). Foundations of Physics, 1, 69; Zurek, W. H. (1981) Physical Review D, 24, 1516. Review: Schlosshauer, M. (2005). Reviews of Modern Physics, 76, 1267. Decoherence solves the practical measurement problem (macroscopic outcomes appear definite) but does not solve the conceptual problem (why this outcome rather than that).

entries.label: Dark Matter & Dark Energy
entries.loc: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, California
entries.hint: Dark matter evidence built from Zwicky’s cluster mass anomaly (1933) through Rubin and Ford’s galactic rotation curves (1970s): stars orbit far too fast at galactic edges given the visible mass alone, demanding ~27% of the universe be non-luminous, non-baryonic matter. In 1998, two independent supernova teams found the universe’s expansion is accelerating, implying ~68% dark energy. No particle candidate for either has been directly detected despite decades of searches, including null WIMP results from LUX-ZEPLIN (2022).
entries.note: Dark energy: Perlmutter, S. et al. (1999). 'Measurements of Ω and Λ from 42 High-Redshift Supernovae.' Astrophysical Journal, 517, 565–586. Riess, A. G. et al. (1998). Astronomical Journal, 116, 1009. Nobel Prize 2011 (Perlmutter, Riess, Schmidt). Dark matter rotation curves: Rubin, V. C. & Ford, W. K. (1970). Astrophysical Journal, 159, 379. LUX-ZEPLIN (2022): no WIMP signal to 9.2 × 10⁻⁴⁸ cm² for 40 GeV. Alternative: MOND (Milgrom 1983) accounts for rotation curves without dark matter but struggles with cluster-scale observations.

entries.label: Manhattan Project & Nuclear Ethics
entries.hint: The development and use of atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki (August 1945, killing ~200,000 people) raised questions that persist: when is it ethical for scientists to build weapons of mass destruction? Meitner refused to participate; Szilard circulated a petition against use on cities; Oppenheimer later lobbied against thermonuclear development, leading to the loss of his security clearance.
entries.note: Szilard, L. et al. (1945). 'Petition to the President of the United States.' Declassified 1961. Oppenheimer security hearing: In the Matter of J. Robert Oppenheimer (1954). Oppenheimer's rehabilitation: the U.S. Department of Energy rescinded the 1954 decision in 2022. Meitner's refusal documented in Sime (1996). The Non-Proliferation Treaty (1968) and subsequent arms-control frameworks are the institutional legacy; nine states currently hold nuclear weapons. The Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists' Doomsday Clock has stood at 90 seconds to midnight since 2023.

entries.label: Beyond the Standard Model
entries.hint: The Standard Model cannot account for gravity, dark matter, the matter-antimatter asymmetry, or neutrino masses (now confirmed experimentally). Supersymmetry predicted superpartner particles accessible at LHC energies; none have been found. Direct dark matter searches have returned null results: LUX-ZEPLIN (2022) found no WIMP signal to unprecedented sensitivity. The Fermilab muon g-2 measurement (2021–2023) shows a ~5σ discrepancy from Standard Model predictions — the strongest current hint of new physics, though the theoretical value is disputed.
entries.note: LHC null results for supersymmetry: ATLAS Collaboration (2018); CMS Collaboration (2018) — no superpartners found up to ~2 TeV. Muon g-2: Abi, B. et al. (Muon g-2 Collaboration) (2021). Physical Review Letters, 126, 141801 (4.2σ); updated to 5.1σ combining with earlier BNL result (2023). Hierarchy problem: the Higgs mass (125 GeV) requires fine-tuning of 34 orders of magnitude against quantum corrections unless new physics intervenes below ~1 TeV. The problem is unsolved.

categories.label: Applications
categories.subtitle: Where the science leaves the lab

entries.label: Nuclear Power Reactors
entries.hint: Chicago Pile-1, built under Fermi's direction beneath a squash court, achieved the first controlled fission chain reaction in December 1942. Modern power reactors use controlled neutron moderation to sustain fission of U-235 or Pu-239, converting the heat to electricity. Nuclear power generates ~10% of global electricity with negligible greenhouse gas emissions but produces long-lived radioactive waste.
entries.note: Fermi, E. et al. (1942). Chicago Pile-1 first chain reaction, 2 December 1942. First commercial power reactor: Calder Hall, UK, 1956. As of 2024, ~440 nuclear reactors operate worldwide. Safety incidents: Three Mile Island (1979, no deaths), Chernobyl (1986, 31 direct deaths, long-term cancer burden disputed), Fukushima (2011, 1 radiation death). Next generation: small modular reactors (SMRs) and Generation IV designs aim for inherent safety and reduced waste volume.

entries.label: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
entries.loc: State University of New York, Stony Brook, New York
entries.hint: Lauterbur showed that adding a gradient magnetic field to NMR allows spatial reconstruction of the resonance signal, creating cross-sectional images of soft tissue without ionising radiation. MRI transformed diagnostic medicine by providing high-contrast anatomical images of the brain, joints and organs — and functional MRI now maps neural activity in real time.
entries.note: Lauterbur, P. C. (1973). 'Image Formation by Induced Local Interactions.' Nature, 242, 190–191. Mansfield, P. & Grannell, P. K. (1973) Journal of Physics C independently showed k-space sampling. Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2003 (Lauterbur & Mansfield). ~100 million MRI scans performed annually worldwide. Functional MRI (fMRI): Ogawa, S. et al. (1990). Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 87, 9868 — BOLD signal (blood-oxygenation-level-dependent) reflects neural activity through neurovascular coupling.

entries.label: Lasers
entries.hint: Maiman's ruby laser exploited stimulated emission — Einstein's 1917 prediction that a photon can trigger an excited atom to emit an identical photon in phase and direction — to produce coherent, monochromatic, collimated light. Lasers now span 25 orders of magnitude in power and underpin fibre-optic communications, surgical tools, manufacturing, gravitational-wave detection, and semiconductor lithography.
entries.note: Maiman, T. H. (1960). 'Stimulated Optical Radiation in Ruby.' Nature, 187, 493–494. Theoretical basis: Einstein, A. (1917). Physikalische Zeitschrift, 18, 121 (stimulated emission). Laser acronym coined by Gordon Gould (1957), whose patent claim was disputed for 28 years. LIGO uses 200 W continuous-wave Nd:YAG lasers; EUV lasers at 13.5 nm enable 3 nm lithography (ASML NXE:3400). Ultra-short pulse lasers (Nobel 2018, CPA technique) achieve 10²³ W/cm² intensities in the lab.

entries.label: Semiconductors & Transistors
entries.loc: Bell Telephone Laboratories, Murray Hill, New Jersey
entries.hint: Shockley, Bardeen, and Brattain's transistor exploited quantum mechanical band theory — electrons in semiconductors occupy discrete energy bands, doping creates controllable charge carriers, and the junction controls current by quantum tunnelling and barrier modulation. The transistor made digital computing possible; modern chips contain billions on a fingernail-sized die.
entries.note: Bardeen, J. & Brattain, W. H. (1948). 'The Transistor, A Semi-Conductor Triode.' Physical Review, 74, 230–231. Nobel Prize 1956 (Bardeen, Brattain, Shockley). The MOSFET (Kahng & Atalla 1960, Bell Labs) became the dominant transistor type. Moore, G. E. (1965). Electronics, 38, 114 predicted transistor density would double every ~2 years — this held for ~60 years. TSMC N2 (2025): ~2 billion transistors/mm². Quantum tunnelling through thin gate oxides is now a limiting factor at sub-2 nm gate lengths.

entries.label: Nuclear Medicine: PET, SPECT & Radiotherapy
entries.hint: PET scanners detect the back-to-back 511 keV gamma rays from positron annihilation to image metabolic activity with millimetre resolution; SPECT uses gamma-emitting isotopes for functional organ imaging; radiotherapy delivers targeted ionising radiation to destroy tumours. All depend on controlled production of specific radionuclides in cyclotrons and reactors.
entries.note: First PET scanner: Ter-Pogossian, M. M. et al. (1975). Radiology, 114, 89–98 (Washington University). Radiotherapy history: Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1895; radiation therapy began in 1896 (Grubbe). ~1.5 million cancer patients receive radiotherapy annually worldwide. PET-CT is the standard staging tool for most solid tumours. Proton therapy (Bragg peak targeting) delivers dose more precisely than photons; ~100 proton therapy centres operate globally as of 2024.

entries.label: GPS & Relativistic Corrections
entries.hint: GPS satellites carry atomic clocks whose ticking rate is affected by both Special Relativity (velocity slows their clocks by 7 μs/day) and General Relativity (weaker gravity speeds their clocks by 45 μs/day). The net +38 μs/day correction is applied continuously; without it, GPS position errors would accumulate at ~10 km/day, making the system useless within hours.
entries.note: Relativistic corrections first calculated: Ashby, N. & Allan, D. W. (1979). Radio Science, 14, 649–669. Verified by GPS Block I satellite experiment (van Flandern, T. C. & Pulkkinen, K. F. 1985, Astrophysical Journal Supplement, 57, 279). Full treatment: Ashby, N. (2003). 'Relativity in the Global Positioning System.' Living Reviews in Relativity, 6, 1. The same corrections apply to Galileo (EU), GLONASS (Russia) and BeiDou (China). Relativistic corrections are also required for atomic clock comparisons used in international timekeeping (TAI).

events.l: Wu: parity violation in beta decay
String theory vs loop quantum gravity
